turns.content: I have a script that generates invalid code using a custom proc-macro, and I'm trying to verify it fails compilation. My current approach wraps the macro output in a String and passes it to trybuild as a file content string, but the tests keep passing even though the generated code shouldn't compile.

That sounds like a common gotcha when dealing with macro expansion testing. The issue is likely that you're passing the raw String output to trybuild instead of letting the compiler actually parse and expand it. You need to use the compile-fail feature on your test module so that the generated code goes through normal compilation, not just string parsing.

Right, I tried using #[test] with a doc comment containing the macro expansion, but trybuild just checks if the doc compiles without actually running my proc-macro. Is there a way to force it to expand?

You're on the right track about the compilation flow. The key is that your test module itself needs to be configured with compile_fail, and you should use the macro directly in the test code rather than just passing a string representation of it.
So I should define a test function where I actually call my proc-macro inline? Like using the generated type or trait impl in the test body itself?

Exactly. You want your test module to import and use the macro-generated types directly, then wrap that whole module with #[test] #[compile_fail]. This forces the compiler to run your proc-macro during compilation and catch any errors it produces.
What about using trybuild's expect-fail feature? Does that work better for macro expansion testing?

trybuild does support compile_fail assertions, but you still need the code to actually be compiled. The difference is that expect-fail will just skip the test if it passes compilation, which defeats your purpose of verifying macro expansion failures.
I'm getting confused about whether I should use serde_json::Value to represent my generated types or keep them as actual Rust types in my test module. Which approach is better for this scenario?
For macro expansion testing, you definitely want the actual Rust types in your test module. Using serde_json::Value would bypass the compiler entirely since it's just data serialization.
Got it. But what if my proc-macro generates something complex like a trait impl that I need to verify compiles correctly?

That's perfect for the compile_fail approach you're planning. Just make sure your test module imports the generated trait and implements it, then wrap everything with #[compile_fail]. The compiler will fail on the invalid parts of your macro output.
One last thing - does trybuild support custom error messages from my proc-macro? Or do I need to parse stderr manually?

trybuild captures the compiler output in the test failure message by default. You can access it via the TestFailure structure, but for basic macro expansion verification, you usually just want the compilation to fail naturally without needing custom parsing.
I'm trying to test a macro that's supposed to fail compilation, but I can't seem to get the expected error message. Should I just use a simple assert!() inside my tests instead?

Using assert!() won't work for verifying that your macro produces an actual compiler error because assert!() only runs at runtime when your code compiles successfully, whereas you need to catch errors that happen during the compilation phase itself. For this specific use case where you want to verify macro expansion behavior by expecting a compile-time failure, trybuild is designed exactly for this purpose since it can parse the output of your build process and match against expected error messages.
Okay, so I need to write a test that expects my Cargo.toml or test manifest to contain specific error text? How do I configure trybuild in my project setup?

You add trybuild as a dev-dependency in your Cargo.toml since it only needs access to the source code during compilation and doesn't need runtime binaries. Then you create a test file where you define a pass or fail attribute on each function, specifying exactly what error message you expect from the macro expansion by using the expected_err() helper method provided by trybuild's crate.

Wait, I thought macros only run at compile time so they can't be tested like regular functions? I assumed all macro logic had to be static and couldn't be verified through test harnesses. Also, what exactly does the pass attribute do since my macro always seems to fail in normal builds?

Macros are executed by the compiler during compilation but their output can vary based on input arguments or conditional logic, which is why you need a dynamic way to verify them rather than relying on static assumptions. The pass and fail attributes tell trybuild whether your test should succeed if no errors occur or succeed only if specific errors appear, so for a macro designed to trigger failures, you'd use the fail attribute with expected_err() to match the exact error string produced by your macro.

That makes sense about the failure mode, but I'm confused why my test isn't running at all. Is trybuild actually compiling my code or just checking the output? And if my macro uses proc-macro attributes that aren't being recognized, does that prevent the test from executing in the first place?

Trybuild compiles your entire crate including your macros and then captures stdout/stderr to compare against what you specified in your tests; it doesn't just check output files without compilation. Proc-macro attributes like #[proc_macro] or #[derive(Deserialize)] can affect whether the compiler recognizes your macro correctly, but trybuild still runs the normal build process so if your proc-macro implementation has a bug preventing expansion, that error will be captured and matched against your expected_err() string.

I think I misunderstood how macros work. I thought once a macro is defined it always produces the same output regardless of context, but clearly from what you're saying, my macro can behave differently depending on the test environment? Isn't that just because I'm using different arguments in each test case? Can I control this behavior through trybuild configuration?

You don't need to change trybuild's configuration since it always runs your code exactly as written; what varies is whether you're testing a macro that conditionally expands based on its inputs or a macro with side effects like logging. If your macro has conditional logic inside it, the pass/fail attributes in your tests determine which branch of behavior you want to verify by selecting specific error messages that only appear under certain conditions.

So I should just write multiple test functions each with different expected_err() strings? But what if my macro generates complex errors involving multiple lines or formatting issues? How does trybuild handle those cases where the output doesn't match exactly line for line?

Trybuild matches against partial error messages using regex patterns so you can specify a substring rather than requiring an exact full-line match, which handles cases where your macro produces complex multi-line errors or formatting variations. You can use regex::Regex to define the expected pattern in your test function arguments, allowing flexibility when dealing with dynamic formatting from macros that might include timestamps, file paths, or variable data in their error output.

That sounds helpful for the flexible matching, but I'm still worried about false positives. What if my macro accidentally produces an error message similar to what I expect by coincidence? Is there a way to make trybuild more strict or verify that the actual compilation failure stems from my specific macro logic rather than unrelated code in my crate?

You can group your tests under a specific module path and use the ignore attribute on unrelated test functions to exclude them, ensuring only the relevant tests run. For stricter verification, you could combine trybuild with custom assertions that check whether the error message contains keywords related to your macro's expected failure condition, like checking if 'name' appears in the error string rather than just matching arbitrary text.

I see, so I should probably add some additional validation logic on top of what trybuild provides? But wait, isn't there a trait that lets me customize how errors are reported without writing extra code? Can I use something like Result::unwrap() or Option::expect() to simplify this further instead of relying solely on regex patterns?

You don't need custom validation logic beyond what trybuild provides since it already handles the core matching; however, you can combine it with assert!() checks for runtime behavior if needed. The trait you're thinking of is likely from a different crate that might offer additional error handling features, but for macro testing specifically, trybuild's expected_err() combined with regex patterns covers most cases without requiring extra code beyond defining your test functions.

Okay, I think I understand the flow now. But just to be sure, what happens if my macro actually expands successfully instead of failing? Does trybuild still run the test or does it skip entirely since there's no error to match against? And how do I distinguish between a macro that silently succeeds versus one that should fail but produces warnings instead of errors?

If your macro expands successfully without producing any errors, then your test will fail because you specified expected_err() expecting failures. Trybuild runs all tests regardless of whether the code compiles or not, so it will report a failure if no error was produced when one was expected. For distinguishing between warnings and errors, you can use trybuild's ignore attribute on specific tests to suppress certain output categories, or use separate test functions with different pass/fail attributes to verify that your macro produces only errors in some cases but warnings in others.

Thanks for clarifying the distinction between warnings and errors. But one last thing - what if I want to test both success and failure scenarios in a single test file? Is there a way to conditionally run different tests based on whether my macro produces an error or not, rather than having separate functions for each case?

You can't dynamically control which tests run based on runtime behavior since trybuild only executes during the compilation phase and doesn't have access to your code's execution state. Instead, you should write separate test functions where each explicitly specifies its expected outcome through pass or fail attributes with appropriate error messages, ensuring that every possible macro behavior is covered by a dedicated test case in your test file.
